From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Vocation founded upon specialized educational training

For other uses, see [Profession (disambiguation)](/wiki/Profession_(disambiguation) "Profession (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/A_farmer_telling_his_family%2C_a_doctor%2C_a_vicar_and_a_lawyer_Wellcome_V0011022.jpg/330px-A_farmer_telling_his_family%2C_a_doctor%2C_a_vicar_and_a_lawyer_Wellcome_V0011022.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_farmer_telling_his_family,_a_doctor,_a_vicar_and_a_lawyer_Wellcome_V0011022.jpg)

A 19th century etching of a farmer consulting with his doctor, vicar and lawyer

A **profession** is a field of [work](/wiki/Work_(human_activity) "Work (human activity)") that has been formally [professionalized](/wiki/Professionalized "Professionalized").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It can be defined as a disciplined group of individuals, [professionals](/wiki/Professional "Professional"), who adhere to ethical standards and are recognized by the public as possessing specialized knowledge and skills grounded in a widely recognized body of learning derived from advanced research, education, and training, and who apply this knowledge and exercise these skills in the interest of others.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Professional occupations are founded upon specialized [educational](/wiki/Education "Education") [training](/wiki/Training "Training"), the purpose of which is to supply disinterested objective counsel and service to others, for direct and definite compensation, wholly apart from expectation of other business gain.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Medieval and early modern tradition recognized only three professions: [divinity](/wiki/Divinity_(academic_discipline) "Divinity (academic discipline)"), [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), and [law](/wiki/Law "Law"),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-perks-p2-6) which were called the **learned professions**.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In some legal definitions, a profession is not a [trade](/wiki/Craft "Craft")[[8]](#cite_note-8) nor an industry.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Some professions change slightly in status and power, but their prestige generally remains stable over time, even if the profession begins to have more required study and formal education.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Disciplines formalized more recently, such as architecture, now have equally long periods of study associated with them.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Although professions may enjoy relatively high status and public prestige, not all professionals earn high salaries, and even within specific professions there exist significant differences in salary. In law, for example, a corporate [defense lawyer](/wiki/Defense_lawyer "Defense lawyer") working on an hourly basis may earn several times what a [prosecutor](/wiki/District_attorney "District attorney") or [public defender](/wiki/Public_defender "Public defender") earns.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The term "profession" is a truncation of the term "liberal profession", which is, in turn, an [Anglicization](/wiki/Anglicization "Anglicization") of the French term *profession libérale*. Originally borrowed by English users in the 19th century, it has been re-borrowed by international users from the late 20th, though the (upper-middle) class overtones of the term do not seem to survive re-translation: "liberal professions" are, according to the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s Directive on Recognition of Professional Qualifications (2005/36/EC), "those practised on the basis of relevant professional qualifications in a personal, responsible and professionally independent capacity by those providing intellectual and conceptual services in the interest of the client and the public".
Under the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), liberal professions are professions that require specialized training and that are regulated by "national governments or professional bodies".[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

A profession arises through the process of [professionalization](/wiki/Professionalization "Professionalization") when any trade or occupation transforms itself:

> "... [through] the development of formal qualifications based upon education, apprenticeship, and examinations, the emergence of regulatory bodies with powers to admit and discipline members, and some degree of [monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly") rights.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Major milestones which may mark an occupation being identified as a profession include:[[6]](#cite_note-perks-p2-6)

1. an occupation becomes a full-time occupation
2. the establishment of a [training](/wiki/Educational_institution "Educational institution") school
3. the establishment of a [university](/wiki/University "University") school
4. the establishment of a local [association](/wiki/Voluntary_association "Voluntary association")
5. the establishment of a national association of [professional ethics](/wiki/Professional_ethics "Professional ethics")
6. the establishment of state [licensing](/wiki/Licensure "Licensure") laws

Applying these milestones to the historical sequence of development in the United States shows [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") achieving professional status first (George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, and Abraham Lincoln all worked as land surveyors before entering politics[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)), followed by [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), [actuarial science](/wiki/Actuarial_science "Actuarial science"), [law](/wiki/Law "Law"), [dentistry](/wiki/Dentistry "Dentistry"), [civil engineering](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering"), [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics"), [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture") and [accounting](/wiki/Accounting "Accounting").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerks19933-17)

With the rise of technology and occupational specialization in the 19th century, other bodies began to claim professional status: [mechanical engineering](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering"), [pharmacy](/wiki/Pharmacy "Pharmacy"), [veterinary medicine](/wiki/Veterinary_medicine "Veterinary medicine"), [psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology"), [nursing](/wiki/Nursing "Nursing"), [teaching](/wiki/Teaching "Teaching"), [librarianship](/wiki/Librarianship "Librarianship"), [optometry](/wiki/Optometry "Optometry") and [social work](/wiki/Social_work "Social work"), each of which could claim, using these milestones, to have become professions by 1900.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

There is considerable agreement about defining the characteristic features of a profession. Professionals have a "professional association, cognitive base, institutionalized training, licensing, [work autonomy](/wiki/Work_autonomy "Work autonomy"), colleague control... (and) code of ethics",[[19]](#cite_note-19) to which Larson adds, "high standards of professional and intellectual excellence",[[20]](#cite_note-20) that "professions are occupations with special power and prestige"[[21]](#cite_note-21) and that they comprise "an exclusive [elite](/wiki/Elite "Elite") group"[[22]](#cite_note-22) in all societies. Joanne Brown defines members of a profession as "workers whose qualities of detachment, autonomy, and group allegiance are more extensive than those found among other groups...their attributes include a high degree of systematic knowledge; strong community orientation and [loyalty](/wiki/Loyalty "Loyalty"); self-regulation; and a system of rewards defined and administered by the community of workers".[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Turner and Hodge have further defined a profession as:

> "a special type of occupation...(possessing) corporate solidarity...prolonged specialized training in a body of abstract knowledge, and a  [collectivity](/wiki/Social_collectivity "Social collectivity") or service orientation...a vocational sub-culture which comprises implicit codes of behavior, generates an [esprit de corps](/wiki/Esprit_de_corps "Esprit de corps") among members of the same profession, and ensures them certain occupational advantages...(also) bureaucratic structures and monopolistic privileges to perform certain types of work...professional literature, legislation, etc."[[24]](#cite_note-24)

A critical characteristic of a profession is the need to cultivate and exercise professional *discretion* - that is, the ability to make case-by-case *judgements* that cannot be determined by an absolute rule or instruction.[[25]](#cite_note-pmid12004638-25)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Regulation")]

Regulatory organisations are typically charged with overseeing a defined industry. Usually they will have two general tasks:

1. creating, reviewing and amending standards expected of individuals and organisations within the industry.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
2. Intervening when there is a reasonable suspicion that a regulated individual or organisation [may not be complying](/wiki/Professional_conduct "Professional conduct") with its obligations.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Originally, any regulation of the professions was [self-regulation](/wiki/Self-regulatory_organization "Self-regulatory organization") through bodies such as the [College of Physicians](/wiki/College_of_Physicians "College of Physicians") or the [Inns of Court](/wiki/Inns_of_Court "Inns of Court"). With the growing role of government, [statutory bodies](/wiki/Statutory_body "Statutory body") have increasingly taken on this role, their members being appointed either by the profession or (increasingly) by the government. Proposals for the introduction or enhancement of statutory regulation may be welcomed by a profession as protecting clients and enhancing its quality and reputation, or as restricting access to the profession and hence enabling higher fees to be charged. It may be resisted as limiting the members' freedom to innovate or to practice as in their professional judgement they consider best.

An example was in 2008, when the British government proposed wide statutory regulation of psychologists. The inspiration for the change was a number of problems in the [psychotherapy](/wiki/Psychotherapy "Psychotherapy") field, but there are various kinds of psychologists including many who have no clinical role, and where the case for regulation was not so clear. [Work psychology](/wiki/Industrial_and_organizational_psychology "Industrial and organizational psychology") brought especial disagreement, with the [British Psychological Society](/wiki/British_Psychological_Society "British Psychological Society") favoring statutory regulation of "occupational psychologists" and the Association of Business Psychologists resisting the statutory regulation of "business psychologists" – descriptions of professional activity which it may not be easy to distinguish.

Besides regulating access to a profession, professional bodies may set [examinations](/wiki/Test_(assessment) "Test (assessment)") of competence and enforce adherence to an [ethical code](/wiki/Ethical_code "Ethical code"). There may be several such bodies for one profession in a single country, an example being the [accountancy bodies](/wiki/British_qualified_accountants "British qualified accountants") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") ([ACCA](/wiki/Association_of_Chartered_Certified_Accountants "Association of Chartered Certified Accountants"), [CAI](/wiki/Chartered_Accountants_Ireland "Chartered Accountants Ireland"), [CIMA](/wiki/Chartered_Institute_of_Management_Accountants "Chartered Institute of Management Accountants"), [CIPFA](/wiki/Chartered_Institute_of_Public_Finance_and_Accountancy "Chartered Institute of Public Finance and Accountancy"), [ICAEW](/wiki/Institute_of_Chartered_Accountants_in_England_and_Wales "Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales") and [ICAS](/wiki/Institute_of_Chartered_Accountants_of_Scotland "Institute of Chartered Accountants of Scotland")), all of which have been given a [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_Charter "Royal Charter"), although their members are not necessarily considered to hold equivalent qualifications, and which operate alongside further bodies ([AAPA](/wiki/Association_of_Authorised_Public_Accountants "Association of Authorised Public Accountants"), [IFA](/wiki/Institute_of_Financial_Accountants "Institute of Financial Accountants"), [CPAA](/wiki/Certified_Public_Accountants_Association "Certified Public Accountants Association")). Another example of a regulatory body that governs a profession is the Hong Kong Professional Teachers Union, which governs the conduct, rights, obligations, and duties of salaried teachers working in educational institutions in Hong Kong.

The [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") profession is highly regulated in some countries (Canada and the United States) with a strict licensing system for [Professional Engineer](/wiki/Professional_Engineer "Professional Engineer") that controls the practice but not in others (UK) where titles and qualifications are regulated [Chartered Engineer](/wiki/Chartered_Engineer "Chartered Engineer") but the practice is not regulated.

Typically, individuals are required by law to be qualified by a local professional body before they are permitted to practice in that profession. However, in some countries, individuals may not be required by law to be qualified by such a professional body in order to practice, as is the case for accountancy in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") (except for auditing and insolvency work which legally require qualification by a professional body). In such cases, qualification by the professional bodies is effectively still considered a prerequisite to practice as most employers and clients stipulate that the individual hold such qualifications before hiring their services. For example, in order to become a fully qualified teaching professional in Hong Kong working in a state or government-funded school, one needs to have successfully completed a [Postgraduate Diploma in Education](/wiki/Postgraduate_Diploma_in_Education "Postgraduate Diploma in Education") ("PGDE") or a bachelor's degree in [Education](/wiki/Education "Education") ("BEd") at an approved tertiary educational institution or university. This requirement is set out by the Educational Department Bureau of Hong Kong, which is the governmental department that governs the Hong Kong education sector.

## Autonomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Autonomy")]

Professions tend to be autonomous, which means they have a high degree of control of their own affairs: "professionals are autonomous insofar as they can make independent judgments about their work".[[28]](#cite_note-28) This usually means "the freedom to exercise their professional judgement."[[29]](#cite_note-29)

However, it also has other meanings. "Professional autonomy is often described as a claim of professionals that has to serve primarily their own interests...this professional autonomy can only be maintained if members of the profession subject their activities and decisions to a critical evaluation by other members of the profession."[[30]](#cite_note-30) The concept of autonomy can therefore be seen to embrace not only judgement, but also self-interest and a continuous process of critical evaluation of ethics and procedures from within the profession itself.

One major implication of professional autonomy is the traditional ban on corporate practice of the professions, especially accounting, architecture, engineering, medicine, and law. This means that in many jurisdictions, these professionals cannot do business through regular for-profit corporations and raise capital rapidly through [initial public offerings or flotations](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering"). Instead, if they wish to practice collectively they must form special business entities such as partnerships or [professional corporations](/wiki/Professional_corporation "Professional corporation"), which feature (1) reduced protection against liability for professional negligence and (2) severe limitations or outright prohibitions on ownership by non-professionals. The obvious implication of this is that all equity owners of the professional business entity must be professionals themselves. This avoids the possibility of a non-professional owner of the firm telling a professional how to do his or her job and thereby protects professional autonomy. The idea is that the *only* non-professional person who should be telling the professional what to do is the *client*; in other words, professional autonomy preserves the integrity of the two-party professional-client relationship. Above this client-professional relationship the profession requires the professional to use their autonomy to follow the rules of ethics that the profession requires. But because professional business entities are effectively locked out of the stock market, they tend to grow relatively slowly compared to public corporations.

## Status, prestige, and power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Status, prestige, and power")]

Main article: [Occupational prestige](/wiki/Occupational_prestige "Occupational prestige")

Professions tend to have a high [social status](/wiki/Social_status "Social status"), regarded by society as highly important.[[31]](#cite_note-31) This high esteem arises primarily from the higher social function of their work. The typical profession involves technical, specialized, and highly skilled work. This skill and experience is often referred to as "professional [expertise](/wiki/Expertise "Expertise")." In the modern era, training for a profession involves obtaining degrees and certifications. Often, entry to the profession is barred without [licensure](/wiki/Licensure "Licensure"). Learning new skills that are required as a profession evolves is called [continuing education](/wiki/Continuing_education "Continuing education"). Standards are set by [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") and associations. Leading professionals tend to police and protect their area of expertise and monitor the conduct of their fellow professionals through associations, national or otherwise. Professionals often exercise a dominating influence over related trades, setting guidelines and standards.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Socially powerful professionals consolidate their power in organizations for specific goals. Working together, they can reduce bureaucratic entanglements and increase a profession's adaptability to the changing conditions of the world.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Sociology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sociology")]

[Émile Durkheim](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Durkheim "Émile Durkheim") argued that professions created a stable society by providing structure separate from the state and the military that was less inclined to create [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") or [anomie](/wiki/Anomie "Anomie") and could create altruism and encourage social responsibility and altruism. This [functionalist](/wiki/Structural_functionalism "Structural functionalism") perspective was extended by [Talcott Parsons](/wiki/Talcott_Parsons "Talcott Parsons") who considered how the function of a profession could change in responses to changes in society.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-34): 17[[35]](#cite_note-35)

[Esther Lucile Brown](/wiki/Esther_Lucile_Brown "Esther Lucile Brown"), an anthropologist, studied various professions starting the 1930s while working with Ralph Hurlin at the [Russell Sage Foundation](/wiki/Russell_Sage_Foundation "Russell Sage Foundation"). She published *Social Work as a Profession* in 1935, and following this publications studying the work of engineers, nurses, medical physicians and lawyers. In 1944, the Department of Studies in the Professions was created at the Russell Sage Foundation with Brown as its head.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36): 183

Theories based on [conflict theories](/wiki/Conflict_theories "Conflict theories") following [Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber "Max Weber") consider how professions can act in the interest of their own group to secure social and financial benefits were espoused by Johnson (*Professions and Powers,* 1972) and Larson (*The Rise of Professionalism*, 1977). One way that a profession can derive financial benefits is limiting the supply of services.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-34): 18

Theories based on discourse, following [Mead](/wiki/George_Herbert_Mead "George Herbert Mead") and applying ideas of [Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre") and [Heidegger](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger") look at how the individual's understanding of reality influence the role of professions. These viewpoints were espoused by [Berger](/wiki/Peter_L._Berger "Peter L. Berger") and [Luckmann](/wiki/Thomas_Luckmann "Thomas Luckmann") (*[The Social Construction of Reality](/wiki/The_Social_Construction_of_Reality "The Social Construction of Reality")*, 1966).[[34]](#cite_note-:0-34): 19

### System of professions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Profession&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: System of professions")]

[Andrew Abbott](/wiki/Andrew_Abbott_(sociologist) "Andrew Abbott (sociologist)") constructed a sociological model of professions in his book *The System of Professions*. Abbott views professions as having *jurisdiction* over the right to carry out tasks with different possession vying for control of jurisdiction over tasks.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

A profession often possesses an *expert knowledge system* which is distinct from the profession itself. This abstract system is often not of direct practical use but is rather optimized for logical consistency and rationality, and to some degree acts to increase the status of the entire profession. One profession may seek control of another profession's jurisdiction by challenging it at this academic level. Abbott argues that in the 1920s the [psychiatric](/wiki/Psychiatry "Psychiatry") profession tried to challenge the legal profession for control over society's response to criminal behavior. Abbott argues the formalization of a profession often serves to make a jurisdiction easier or harder to protect from other jurisdictions: general principles making it harder for other professions to gain jurisdiction over one area, clear boundaries preventing encroachment, fuzzy boundaries making it easier for one profession to take jurisdiction over other tasks.

Professions may expand their jurisdiction by other means. Lay education on the part of professions as in part an attempt to expand jurisdiction by imposing a particular understanding on the world (one in which the profession has expertise). He terms this sort of jurisdiction *public jurisdiction*. *Legal jurisdiction* is a monopoly created by the state legislation, as applies to law in many nations.
